items.prompt_md: In the one-pass solution to Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock, what does the window's left edge (`lo`) represent?
options: The cheapest price seen so far
The maximum profit found so far
The index of the last trade
items.answer: The cheapest price seen so far
items.explanation_md: `lo` tracks the lowest price encountered before (or at) the current day. It only ever moves forward, and only when today's price is cheaper than everything before it.

items.prompt_md: You're scanning an array once and need the best result measured against the best value seen so far — no shrinking condition, just a running extreme updated as you go. Which approach fits?
options: Slide a window whose left edge jumps forward whenever a new best is found, while the right edge always advances
Sort the array first, then binary search for each element
Keep a hash map of every value seen and look up a target complement
Try every pair with two nested loops
items.answer: Slide a window whose left edge jumps forward whenever a new best is found, while the right edge always advances
items.explanation_md: One running extreme + one forward pass is the fixed-window fingerprint. Sorting or nested loops both throw away the fact that the answer only depends on a single running min/max, and a hash map answers a different question (membership, not "best so far").

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the one-pass solution to Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock?
items.explanation_md: One scan of the prices, updating two running variables (`lo` and `best`) per element, gives O(n) time and O(1) extra space — no nested loop, no sort.
